concepts.title: Direction of friction in pure rolling
concepts.conceptBody:
Direction of friction in pure rolling

Let us assume a force $\text{F}$ applied on a height ' $\text{h}$ ' above its centre $\text{O}$. 

Then acceleration of the centre of mass of the body is given by

and torque due to the force $\text{F}$

$\tau=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{h}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}} \alpha=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{h}$

\(\therefore \alpha=\frac{\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}}}\) ...(2)

.Now, $\text{a}=\text{R}\alpha $ for pure rolling

From (1) & (2)

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \Rightarrow & \frac{\text{F}}{\text{M}}=\text{R}\left( \frac{\text{F}\cdot \text{h}}{{{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}} \right) \\ \therefore & {{\text{h}}_{0}}=\frac{{{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}}{\text{MR}} \\ \end{array}$

In this case force exerted by friction $\left( {{f}_{s}} \right)$ will be zero. This is because fs starts acting only when there is relative motion between two surfaces in contact.

Now, let us consider different cases and determine the direction of ${{f}_{s}}$.

(i) If $\text{h}>{{\text{h}}_{0}}$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ then }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & {} \\ {} & \alpha \mathbf{R}>\text{a} \\ \end{array}$

this means that net motion of body with respect to surface will be in the backward direction. Hence ${{\text{f}}_{\text{s}}}$ will start acting in the forward direction to oppose this relative motion.

Hence, ${{f}_{s}}$ will be in the direction of $F$. (ii) $\text{h}<{{\text{h}}_{0}}$

In this case a $>\alpha \mathbf{R}$. Thus the body will have a tendency to slip forward and hence, force of friction will act in backward direction.

concepts.title: Instantaneous axis of rotation
concepts.conceptBody:
Instantaneous Axis of Rotation

There is another term which will be covered in the same article called Instantaneous centre of rotation (ICR). 

IAR : It is the axis about which the motion of a rigid body undergoing plane motion is assumed to be pure rotatory with same $\omega $ and $\alpha $ as that of body about its COM axis. {This is always perpendicular to the plane of motion of rigid body.}

ICR : It is the point of intersections of IAR with the plane of motion of rigid body.

ICR is the centre (of the circle) about which all particles of the rigid body are going in circle with same $\omega $ and $\alpha $, but in different radius equal to the distance from ICR.

Note : ICR and IAR are instantaneously at rest, i.e. at an instant their velocity is zero, but their acceleration may or may not be zero.

To find the position of ICR 

Consider the body shown in figure where the line of action of the velocities ${{\vec{v}}_{A}}$ and ${{\vec{v}}_{B}}$ are known. Draw perpendicular at A and B to these lines of action. The point of intersection of these perpendiculars as shown locates the IC at the instant considered.

In pure rolling, IAR passes through the point of contact of the surface and body. {This is because the point $\text{O}$ is at rest as $\text{v}=\text{R}\omega $ }

 
 

Now, ICR is used to know the direction and magnitude of the net velocity of any point of the body.

For example, if we have to calculate velocity of point $\text{A}$, then join point $\text{O}$ and $\text{A}$ and the direction perpendicular to this line OA will give us the value of net velocity and the magnitude of velocity will be given by ${{\text{v}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\omega \left( \text{OA} \right)$.

Now, $\text{OA}=\text{R}\sqrt{2}$

$\therefore {{\text{v}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\omega \text{R}\sqrt{2}$

Similarly, velocity of centre of sphere ${{\text{v}}_{\text{net }\!\!~\!\!\text{ C}}}=\omega \left( \text{OC} \right)=\omega \text{R}=\text{v }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\{$ as $\text{v}=\omega \text{R}\}$ in the forward direction.

Some points 

(i) When $\text{v}>\omega \text{R}$, ICR lies below point of contact.

(ii) When $\text{v}<\omega \text{R}$, ICR lies above point of contact.

How to find ICR when the velocities are parallel or anti-parallel 

When the velocities of points $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ are parallel and have known magnitudes ${{\text{v}}_{\text{A}}}$ and ${{\text{v}}_{\text{B}}}$ then the location of the IC is determined by proportional triangles as shown in figure.

In both the case,

${{\text{r}}_{\text{A'},\text{IC}}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{A}}}}{\omega }$

and

${{\text{r}}_{\text{B},\text{IC}}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}_{\text{B}}}}{\omega }$

In Fig. (a)

and in Fig. (b)

${{\text{r}}_{\text{A},\text{IC}}}+{{\text{r}}_{\text{B},\text{IC}}}=\text{d}{{\text{r}}_{\text{B},\text{IC}}}-{{\text{r}}_{\text{A},\text{IC}}}=\text{d}$

As a special case, if the body is translating, ${{\text{v}}_{\text{A}}}={{\text{v}}_{\text{B}}}$ and the IC would be located at infinity, in which case $\omega =0$

concepts.title: Rolling motion on inclined plane
concepts.conceptBody:
Rolling motion on Inclined Plane 

Consider the situation shown in figure. Friction will be acting up the plane. Thus equations can be formed as below.

$\text{ from }\left( 2 \right),{{\text{f}}_{\text{s}}}\text{R}={{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}\frac{\text{a}}{\text{R}}\Rightarrow {{\text{f}}_{\text{s}}}=\frac{{{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}\text{a}}{{{\text{R}}^{2}}}$

$Mg\text{sin}\theta =\left( M+\frac{{{I}_{CM}}}{{{R}^{2}}} \right)a$

$\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}=\left(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}} \frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{R}^2}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{mg} \sin \theta}{1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}}}\right)}$

$\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{g}} \leq \mu \mathrm{N}$

$\therefore \quad \mu_{\min }=\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{s}}}{\mathrm{N}}\right)$

$\therefore \quad \mu_{\min } \geq \frac{\tan \theta}{1+\left(\frac{\mathrm{mR}^2}{\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{CM}}}\right)}$

$f_s=(m g \sin \theta)$

Now, from equation (4); we can interpret acceleration for different bodies.

$\begin{array}{l} {{\text{a}}_{\text{ring }}}={{\text{a}}_{\text{hollow }\text{ cylinder }}}=\frac{1}{2}\text{ gsin}\theta \\ {{\text{a}}_{\text{disc }}}={{\text{a}}_{\text{solid }\text{ cylinder }}}=\frac{2}{3}\left( \text{gsin}\theta \right) \\ {{\text{a}}_{\text{solid }\text{ sphere }}}=\frac{5}{7}\text{ gsin}\theta ,{{\text{a}}_{\text{hollow }\text{ sphere }}}=\frac{3}{5}\text{ gsin}\theta \\ {{\text{a}}_{\text{ss}}}>{{\text{a}}_{\text{d}}}={{\text{a}}_{\text{sc}}}>{{\text{a}}_{\text{hs}}}>{{\text{a}}_{\text{r}}}={{\text{a}}_{\text{hc}}} \\ \end{array}$

Acceleration down the planle depends only the shape of the body and inclination $\theta $ and is independent of size of body.

Note : 

(1) When the friction coefficient is insufficient $\left( {} \right.$ i.e. $\left. \mu <\frac{\text{tan}\theta }{1+\frac{\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}}{{{\text{I}}_{\text{CM}}}}} \right)$ the slipping takes place and pure rolling cannot take place.

Hence kinetic friction acceleration acts up the plane.

(2) In case of pure rolling on an inclined plane acceleration always acts down the plane.

(3) In case of pure rolling friction acts up the plane and is static

Every body once in pure rolling tries to sustain the pure rolling motion.

examples.title: The free end of a thread wound on a bobbin is passed round a nail A hammered into the wall. The thread is pulled at a constant velocity. Assuming pure rolling of bobbin, find the velocity $v_0 \mid$ of the centre of the bobbin at the instant when the thread forms an angle a with the vertical.

In the figure shown mass of both, the spherical body and block is $\mathrm{m}$. Moment of inertia of the spherical body about centre of mass is $2 m R^2$. The spherical body rolls on the horizontal surface. There is no slipping at any surfaces in contact. The ratio of kinetic energy of the spherical body to that of block is
A rolling body is kept on a plank $B$. There is sufficient friction between $A$ and $B$ and no friction between $B$ and the inclined plane. Then body:

A thin ring, a disk and an annular cylinder, of same mass $M$, are released from a point $3.6 \mathrm{~m}$ from the ground up an inclined plane of $30^{\circ}$ degree inclination. The ring and the disk have the same radius $R$. Times taken by the ring and disk to reach the ground are in the ratio,

A tangential force of $20 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied on a cylinder of mass $4 \mathrm{~kg}$ and moment of inertia $\mid 0.02 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 \mid$ about its own axis. If the cylinder rolls without slipping, then linear acceleration of its centre of mass will be

A thick walled hollow sphere has outer radius $\mathrm{R}$. It rolls down an inclined plain Without slipping and its speed at the bottom is v. If the inclined plane is frictionless and the sphere slides down without rolling, its speed at the bottom will be $5 \mathrm{v} / 4$. What is radius of gyration of the sphere.

A cylinder A rolls without slipping on a plank $B$. The velocities of center of the cylinder and that of the plank are $4 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ respectively in same direction, with respect to the ground. Find the angular velocity of the cylinder (in rad/s) if its radius is $1 \mathrm{~m}$.

A solid sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is set into motion on a rough horizontal surface with a linear speed $v_0$ in forward direction and an angular velocity $\omega_0=v_0 / 2 R$ in counter coefficient of friction is $\mu$, then find the time after which sphere starts pure rolling.

A hollow sphere of mass $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ is kept on a rough horizontal surface. A force of $10 \mathrm{~N}$ is applied at the centre of the sphere as shown in the figure. Find the minimum value $\mu$ so that the sphere starts pure rolling. (Take $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
A spherical ball rolls on a table without slipping. Then the fraction of its total energy associated with rotation is
A solid sphere, disc and solid cylinder all of the same mass and made of the same material are allowed to roll down (from rest) on an inclined plane, then
A solid homogeneous sphere is moving on a rough horizontal surface, partly rolling and partly sliding. During the king of motion of the sphere.
